risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization and packaging was organized by an independent statistician at the host site, using the SPSS software Rand function." Comment: This explicitly states that the allocation sequence was generated using a random-number function, which is a truly random method. The additional description of numbered envelopes and blocks reflects concealment/implementation, not the sequence generation itself; however, the Rand function is sufficient to judge low risk for this domain.

Quote: "To allow participants to be recruited at satellite centres without computer links, randomization was by sealed envelope. Numbered envelopes containing a random treatment allocation were provided to each site. On enrolment participants selected their envelope from a large batch and signed it before breaking the seal to reveal their allocation." Comment: This explicitly describes a numbered envelope system where the assignment was hidden until the envelope was opened, which prevents foreknowledge of the next allocation. Although the passage does not use the exact phrase SNOSE, the combination of numbered envelopes, sealing, and opening only after enrolment is sufficient to judge allocation concealment as low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was an open-label parallel-group trial" and "we could not include placebos to allow a no-treatment condition or to blind treatment allocation". Comment: Participants and treating personnel were explicitly not blinded, and the main outcomes included smoking cessation and unwanted symptoms/adverse events, which could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of treatment assignment (e.g. expectations, support, reporting, or adherence).

Quote: "Blind follow-ups were not considered feasible, due to patients usually discussing their treatment when contacted." and "The primary outcome measure ... defined as a self-report ... and recording an ECO level of <10 parts per million (p.p.m.) at session 7 and at 6 months." Comment: Outcome assessment was not blinded, but the main smoking cessation outcomes were CO-verified and therefore largely objective/standardized; the lack of blinded follow-up is unlikely to have influenced the ECO measurement itself. The subjective self-report component could be influenced, but the biochemical verification reduces the risk of detection bias for the primary abstinence outcomes.

Quote: "The two follow-ups for the primary outcome (session 7, 26 weeks) were completed by 659 (61.5%) participants and this rate did not differ significantly between treatment groups (χ2(2) = 3.33, P = 0.19). Of the 412 participants not completing both follow-ups, 241 (58.5%) reported smoking at one follow-up which they did complete... only for the remaining 171 (16.0% of all participants) ... was it necessary to assume continued smoking. This gave a primary outcome completion rate of 84%. Again, this rate did not differ between treatment groups." Comment: Although some participants missed follow-up, the paper reports balanced completion across groups, explains that most missing participants had already reported smoking, and only 16% required an assumption of continued smoking. Overall attrition appears reasonable and unlikely to bias the treatment comparison.
